chunk_summary: This section outlines a structured discharge nutrition readiness checklist and safety bundle for preterm/LBW/ELBW infants, emphasizing measurable feeding plans, caregiver competency, and early post-discharge monitoring to prevent growth failure.
learning_objectives: Understand the components of a discharge nutrition readiness checklist for preterm/LBW/ELBW infants.
Learn how to create measurable feeding plans, including intake prescriptions and top-up triggers.
Recognize caregiver competency requirements for safe feeding and storage practices.
Identify early post-discharge monitoring protocols to ensure infant growth and safety.
key_facts: Most post-discharge growth failure is due to unmeasured intake, poor transfer, or unclear escalation pathways.
Minimum pre-discharge elements include observing a full feeding cycle and documenting positioning, stability, endurance, and feeding time.
Target daily fluid intake should be specified in mL/kg/day, with feeds per day and mL per feed clearly prescribed.
Breastfeeding top-up triggers include prolonged feeds (>30 minutes), reduced urine output, or poor weight trends.
India’s SNCU follow-up guidance recommends the first home visit within 24 hours post-discharge.
reference_quote: India’s SNCU follow-up guidance can be used as a scaffold (day of discharge counted as day 1, first home visit within 24 hours, then early scheduled contacts).
document_key_topics: Caregiver competency in feeding and storage
Structured feeding plans and escalation pathways
